quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "d/dx[sin x] = cos x"?

options.text: Change of base: logₐ x = ln x / ln a, so d/dx[logₐ x] = 1/(x ln a)
Divide the plate into thin horizontal strips of width dh; each strip has approximately constant pressure ρgh and area w(h)·dh
Priority for choosing u: Logarithmic > Inverse trig > Algebraic > Trigonometric > Exponential; u is differentiated, dv is integrated
Derived from the limit definition using sin(x+h) = sin x cos h + cos x sin h and special limits

quiz_questions.explanation: "d/dx[sin x] = cos x" is defined as: Derived from the limit definition using sin(x+h) = sin x cos h + cos x sin h and special limits. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "d/dx[cos x] = -sin x"?

options.text: In economics: marginal cost C'(x), marginal revenue R'(x), and marginal profit P'(x) = R'(x) - C'(x) at production level x
d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n; includes x⁻¹ = 1/x, x^(1/2) = √x, x^(2/3), etc.
∫u dv = uv - ∫v du; derived from the product rule: d(uv) = u dv + v du
Derived similarly; the negative sign reflects that cosine decreases when sine is positive

quiz_questions.explanation: "d/dx[cos x] = -sin x" is defined as: Derived similarly; the negative sign reflects that cosine decreases when sine is positive. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "d/dx[tan x] = sec²x"?

options.text: If f'(x) = 0 for all x in an interval, MVT proves f must be a constant function on that interval
Derived from d/dx[sin x / cos x] using the quotient rule; always positive, so tan x has no horizontal tangents
μ = E[X] = ∫₋∞^∞ x·f(x)dx; the 'center of mass' of the probability distribution
d/dx[f(x)/g(x)] = [f'(x)g(x) - f(x)g'(x)] / [g(x)]²; 'low d-high minus high d-low over low squared'

quiz_questions.explanation: "d/dx[tan x] = sec²x" is defined as: Derived from d/dx[sin x / cos x] using the quotient rule; always positive, so tan x has no horizontal tangents. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "d/dx[sec x] = sec x tan x"?

options.text: [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a); the average rate of change over [a,b]; the slope of the line connecting endpoints
f⁻¹ reverses f: if f(a) = b, then f⁻¹(b) = a; domain of f⁻¹ = range of f, and vice versa
Numerical approximation: start at (x₀,y₀), step by yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + f(xₙ,yₙ)·Δx with step size Δx
Derived from d/dx[1/cos x] using the chain rule; undefined where cos x = 0

quiz_questions.explanation: "d/dx[sec x] = sec x tan x" is defined as: Derived from d/dx[1/cos x] using the chain rule; undefined where cos x = 0. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Co-function pattern"?

options.text: When v(t) = 0 AND v(t) changes sign; the particle reverses its direction of travel
f''(x) > 0; the graph curves upward like a cup; the slope is increasing
F(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t)dt; represents the accumulated area from a to x; its derivative is f(x) by FTC Part 1
Derivatives of 'co' functions (cos, cot, csc) all have negative signs compared to their counterparts (sin, tan, sec)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Co-function pattern" is defined as: Derivatives of 'co' functions (cos, cot, csc) all have negative signs compared to their counterparts (sin, tan, sec). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Trigonometric Derivatives is accurate?

options.text: The key insight is that pressure increases with depth, so different parts of the plate experience different pressures.
Trigonometric Derivatives form a closed set of six interconnected derivatives.
Arc length of y = f(x) from a to b: L = ∫ₐᵇ √(1 + [f'(x)]²)dx.
For definite integrals: ∫ₐᵇ u dv = [uv]ₐᵇ - ∫ₐᵇ v du.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Trigonometric Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Trigonometric Derivatives?

options.text: The fundamental pair: d/dx[sin x] = cos x and d/dx[cos x] = -sin x (derived from the limit definition using lim(h→0) sin(h)/h = 1).
The indefinite integral ∫f(x)dx = F(x) + C, where C is the constant of integration (since derivatives of constants are zero).
When k > 0: exponential growth (population, investment); when k < 0: exponential decay (radioactive decay, drug clearance).
For logarithms: d/dx[ln x] = 1/x and d/dx[logₐ x] = 1/(x ln a).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Trigonometric Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Trigonometric Derivatives?

options.text: Choose u and dv from the integrand using the LIATE priority: Logarithmic, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential (choose u from earlier in the list).
From these, using quotient rules: d/dx[tan x] = sec²x, d/dx[cot x] = -csc²x, d/dx[sec x] = sec x tan x, d/dx[csc x] = -csc x cot x.
Key consequences include: (1) if f'(x) = 0 for all x in (a,b), then f is constant, (2) if f'(x) = g'(x) for all x, then f(x) = g(x) + C, (3) if f'(x) > 0 on (a,b), then f is strictly increasing.
Limits and Continuity are the foundation of calculus.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Trigonometric Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Trigonometric Derivatives?

options.text: If f'(t) is the rate of water flowing into a tank, then ∫₀ᵀ f'(t)dt is the net change in water volume.
These formulas arise from the Pythagorean theorem applied to infinitesimal arc segments: ds = √(dx² + dy²) = √(1 + (dy/dx)²)dx.
Logarithmic Differentiation systematically uses properties of logarithms to simplify complex differentiation.
Note the pattern: 'co' functions have negative derivatives.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Trigonometric Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Trigonometric Derivatives?

options.text: Trigonometric Derivatives form a closed set of six interconnected derivatives.
Continuous compound interest follows A = Pe^(rt).
Visualizing MVT helps interpret derivative graphs and connect graphical and analytical representations.
A Riemann sum divides [a,b] into n subintervals and approximates the area using rectangles: Σᵢf(xᵢ*)Δx.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Trigonometric Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Trigonometric Derivatives?

options.text: Simple harmonic motion: position x(t) = A sin(ωt) gives velocity v(t) = Aω cos(ωt) and acceleration a(t) = -Aω² sin(ωt), showing oscillatory behavior
Navigation: calculating bearing angles using arctan(Δy/Δx) and differentiating to find rate of angle change for tracking systems
Control systems engineering: inverse tangent functions model phase shifts in frequency response, with derivatives indicating sensitivity to frequency changes
Engineering error analysis: propagation of measurement uncertainty through formulas using differentials to estimate how input errors affect computed results

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Trigonometric Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Trigonometric Derivatives?

options.text: Algorithm analysis in computer science: O(n log n) sorting algorithms (merge sort) outperform O(n²) algorithms (bubble sort) for large datasets
AC electrical circuits: voltage V(t) = V₀ sin(ωt) has derivative V'(t) = V₀ω cos(ωt), the rate of voltage change used in capacitor/inductor analysis
Climate data: identifying periods of warming (increasing temperature trend) vs. cooling using derivative analysis of temperature time series
Coulomb's law: electric force F = kq₁q₂/r² → ∞ as distance r → 0, creating a vertical asymptote that reflects the impossibility of zero separation between charges

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Trigonometric Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Trigonometric Derivatives applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Error analysis in numerical methods: understanding limits helps predict how rounding errors accumulate as step sizes approach zero in computer calculations
Engineering error analysis: propagation of measurement uncertainty through formulas using differentials to estimate how input errors affect computed results
Financial markets: the 'Greeks' (delta, gamma, theta) in options pricing are derivatives that measure how option value changes with respect to stock price, rate of change, and time
Wave mechanics: the derivative of a sine wave gives its phase-shifted cosine companion, fundamental to understanding interference and diffraction patterns

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Trigonometric Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Trigonometric Derivatives?

options.text: Simple harmonic motion: position x(t) = A sin(ωt) gives velocity v(t) = Aω cos(ωt) and acceleration a(t) = -Aω² sin(ωt), showing oscillatory behavior
Navigation: calculating bearing angles using arctan(Δy/Δx) and differentiating to find rate of angle change for tracking systems
Medical imaging: CT scans reconstruct 3D images by computing rates of change of X-ray attenuation through tissue from multiple angles
Medical imaging: CT scan data integrates cross-sectional areas to compute organ volumes, aiding diagnosis and surgical planning

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Trigonometric Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Trigonometric Derivatives in practice?

options.text: Coulomb's law: electric force F = kq₁q₂/r² → ∞ as distance r → 0, creating a vertical asymptote that reflects the impossibility of zero separation between charges
AC electrical circuits: voltage V(t) = V₀ sin(ωt) has derivative V'(t) = V₀ω cos(ωt), the rate of voltage change used in capacitor/inductor analysis
Aquarium design: the total force on viewing windows increases dramatically with depth, requiring thicker glass for deeper tanks
Rocket propulsion: as fuel is burned, the rocket becomes lighter, changing the force equation—integration handles the continuously varying mass

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Trigonometric Derivatives.
